petitioner_background: The petitioner is a self-represented Chinese Peking Opera Actor seeking classification as an employment-based immigrant under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Act as an alien of extraordinary ability in the arts. He has been residing in the United States since December 19, 1995, and was the beneficiary of an approved P-3 nonimmigrant petition authorizing him to work as an artist/entertainer from April 7, 2003 to May 1, 2004, filed by the Taishan International Culture Exchange Center. He previously worked as a professional Peking Opera actor in the Henan Provincial Peking Opera Troupe from September 1988 to November 1995. The petition was filed on July 28, 2003.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted six award certificates: (1) a 'Certificate of Honor' stating he won third place in the First Professional Competition of Middle-aged and Young Performers of Wuhan City (1982); (2) a 'Certificate of Honor' for the Excellent Performance Award in the 1987 National Television Grand Selection Contest of Young Peking Opera Performers; (3) a 'Certificate of Performance' for participation in the Show of '91 Chinese Tourist Art Festival and Guangdong Carnival; (4) a certificate for winning the 'Plum Blossom Prize of Chinese Traditional Opera' (1991); (5) a 'Certificate of Award' for the First Place Bud Award in the Second Joint Show of Secondary Art Schools of Shandong Province (1992); and (6) a certificate stating the petitioner was awarded for his contribution in the Third National Competition of Peking Opera (1995).
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The translations accompanying the award certificates were not certified as required by 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3). Regarding items 1, 2, and 5, there was no indication the petitioner faced competition from throughout his field rather than his approximate age group, and a youth award offers no meaningful comparison between the petitioner and established performing arts professionals; item 2 also reflected provincial rather than national or international recognition. For item 3, there was no evidence the certificate was a nationally recognized award for excellence rather than simply an acknowledgment of participation. For item 6, the award recipient named in the non-certified English translation was not the petitioner. There was no evidence of publicity surrounding the awards or evidence they enjoyed a significant level of recognition, and the petitioner provided no documentation from awarding entities or print media to establish the awards are nationally recognized, nor official comprehensive results from the competitions.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence of his membership in the 'World Association of Beauty Culture' and the Association of Chinese Artists. The translation accompanying his membership card from the Association of Chinese Artists listed his membership grade as 'Second-Class Performer of State.' His 'Working Unit' was listed as the Henan Provincial Peking Opera Troupe.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The record included no evidence of the bylaws or official admission requirements for these associations showing that they require outstanding achievement in the performing arts. The petitioner did not explain how being a 'Second-Class Performer' indicates that he is one of that small percentage who have risen to the very top of the performing arts field. The AAO further noted that the petitioner's 'Working Unit,' the Henan Provincial Peking Opera Troupe, is a provincial organization rather than a national or international organization. There was no evidence demonstrating that admission to membership required outstanding achievement or that the petitioner was evaluated by national or international experts in consideration of his admission to membership. The AAO also explained that membership requirements based on employment, minimum education, test scores, recommendations, or payment of dues do not satisfy this criterion, and members must be selected at the national or international level rather than local or regional level.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted a one-sentence 'Notarial Certificate' stating that he 'worked as a professional Peking Opera actor in Henan Provincial Peking Opera Troupe from September 1988 to November 1995.' This certificate was issued by a Notary in the Henan Province Notary Public Office, rather than by an official of the Henan Provincial Peking Opera Troupe.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The petitioner must establish the nature of his role within the entire organization and the reputation of the organization. The record included no evidence showing that this opera troupe has earned a distinguished reputation when compared to other Peking Opera troupes throughout China. Nor was there evidence of the petitioner's individual importance to the troupe's overall success. The petitioner did not establish that he performed in a leading or critical role for a distinguished organization, or that his involvement earned him sustained national or international acclaim.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO concurred with the director's finding that the petitioner failed to demonstrate he meets at least three of the criteria at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3). The AAO emphasized that acclaim must be sustained, and despite the petitioner residing in the United States since December 19, 1995 (more than seven years before filing), there was no evidence he had been involved in any Peking Opera work during the last ten years — astonishingly, no evidence of performance work even during his authorized P-3 period. The record did not establish that the petitioner distinguished himself as a performer to such an extent that he achieved sustained national or international acclaim or that he is within the small percentage at the very top of his field. Additionally, beyond the director's decision, the AAO noted the record contained no clear evidence under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(5) that the petitioner is coming to the United States to continue work in his area of expertise, such as letters from prospective employers, prearranged commitments, or a statement detailing plans to continue his work. Each ground was considered an independent and alternative basis for denial.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act
Spencer Enterprises, Inc. v. United States, 229 F. Supp. 2d 1025, 1043 (E.D. Cal. 2001), aff'd, 345 F.3d 683 (9th Cir. 2003)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: Procedural issues: (1) the translations accompanying the petitioner's award certificates were not certified as required by 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3); (2) one award certificate (item 6, the Third National Competition of Peking Opera) named a recipient in the non-certified English translation who was not the petitioner, raising a discrepancy about whether the award was actually issued to him; (3) the petitioner failed to submit the clear evidence required under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(5) that he is coming to the United States to continue work in his area of expertise.
